title: Seeking Clarity
html:
Lucy turns to look at him. The katana is in her hand and her updated profile is still fading from the air above the pedestal, and she has the expression of someone who has finished waiting for the next thing to arrive and has decided to ask a direct question instead.

"Can you explain what all of this is?"

"I also have no idea," Lucas says. He sits with his back against the wall, arms resting on his knees. "I got it on the 21st after killing a mutated fish. The system didn't communicate right away — just the broadcast sound, and I dismissed it the same as you probably did. Then I ate the fish and the profile screen appeared. My working theory is that it's tied to interacting with something that's part of the mutation. If you never tried to kill anything and never engaged with the system in any way, you'd stay completely unaware it existed." He pauses. "Which is apparently what happened to you for three days."

"I was hiding," she says, not defensively.

"I know. It makes sense." He does not mean it as criticism and she can tell, because she lets it go. She looks at her own profile — still up, faintly — and then at his face.

"Here's something I don't understand, though. In the KTV, when people started turning, they didn't become players. They became zombies. So why do some people become players and others zombies, when mutated animals just become stronger versions of what they already were? What's the difference?"

Lucas looks at the ceiling for a moment. It is a genuinely good question and he has not worked through that part yet. He is not going to pretend otherwise. "Tell me about the party. Was there a lot of drinking?"

"Why does that matter?"

"I have a theory. Alcohol interferes with processing. If the system announced itself the way it announced to me — just a voice in the head, very easy to dismiss — then someone who was already drunk when it happened might not have been conscious enough to register what was being offered. Their subconscious heard the sound. Their reasoning didn't catch it."

Lucy's expression shifts. "So the people who were drinking when the announcement went off..."

"Became zombies," he says. "Maybe. I'm not certain. But it's the most consistent explanation I can find. The people who were sober and present when it happened became players, because they could actually process the system's offer — even if they had no idea what they were accepting. Mutated animals skip that entirely. There's no decision for them. They just get the upgrade because they don't have consciousness to complicate things. But for humans, the capacity to accept the system might have depended on how clear-headed you were at midnight."

Lucy is quiet for a moment. She turns it over. "That fits. The ones in the KTV who turned first — they'd been drinking the longest. I noticed that even at the time, I just didn't have a frame for it." She looks down at the katana in her hand. "So we might be giving experience points to zombies too. If they kill us."

"Probably yes," Lucas says.

She absorbs this with less alarm than he expected. "And you think both zombies and mutated animals have their own system, just without the ability to use it the way we can?"

"The system gives levels to everything it tracks. What a zombie or a mutated dog can do with those levels compared to what we can do — I don't know. But the EXP values scale with level, which means the system is measuring something in them. Whether they can allocate stats or read a profile is a different question." He pauses. "I've been treating it as: zombies get stronger over time the same way we do, just without being able to direct it."

Lucy nods slowly and looks at her profile again. Her Int line is sitting at 17, three below the adult average. Her expression does something small and closed when she reads it.

"I'm not even at average," she says. "That would explain a few things about school."

"Int at 25 here," Lucas offers. "Five above average. It hasn't helped me understand the system any faster."

"That's not reassuring."

"It wasn't meant to be."

She makes a short sound that is more surprise than amusement. She looks at him with a slightly different expression than before, something more settled in it. "How old are you?"

"Sixteen. I turned sixteen three days ago."

"Same here," she says. "I turned sixteen last month. So I'm older." She applies the specific satisfaction that people reserve for age gaps measured in weeks when both parties are technically the same age.

Something catches up with Lucas a moment after she says it. She reads it on his face. "Wait. Your birthday was on the 21st?"

"Ahem. Yes."

She stares at him. "You turned sixteen the night the apocalypse started?"

"There was birthday cake," he says. "And then a fish. And then a zombie kicked my back door in."

"A birthday party zombie hunt," she says, with the tone of someone filing this for future use. "With 25 Int, you probably had them organized into alphabetical order."

"I was alone," he says. "There was no party."

"Oh." She recalibrates. "What about your parents? Were they not home?"

"They passed away when I was ten," he says. He says it the way he has learned to say it over six years, which is plainly and without much weight on either side. It is a fact. It was a long time ago.

Lucy goes quiet. The room holds it for a moment. "I'm sorry," she says.

"It was a long time ago," he says, which is the answer he always gives because it is true and because it is the answer that makes the conversation easiest for everyone involved. The silence that follows is the specific kind that comes when neither person has a clean next line. It sits between them for a few seconds longer than is comfortable.

He clears his throat. The timing of it is slightly off. She looks at him and something about the combination — the silence, the cough, the fact that neither of them managed to say anything useful — catches her sideways and she makes a sound that is technically a laugh. He makes one shortly after. The room's atmosphere changes in the way it does when whatever was creating the pressure has passed.

"Right." Lucas checks his phone. 12:24 pm. "We need to move. A few hours of light left and I want to be inside the city before dark."

"The city," Lucy says. "There'll be zombies everywhere in there."

"Yes. That's why I want to go." He leans forward slightly, elbows on his knees. "The outskirts are mostly clear. Low zombie density means low EXP, which means slow leveling while everything inside gets stronger by the day. The city center has the highest concentration — which also means the most EXP available in one place. If I go in while I'm still faster-growing than what's in there, I can stay ahead of the curve. On top of that, mutated animals around the city's edge — near slaughterhouses, near the river — are leveling by fighting each other the same way the fish in the lake were. A high-level mutated animal is more dangerous than a zombie because it has hunting instincts. Zombies react to noise and movement. They don't plan. I can manage zombies. A Level 10 mutated dog is a different problem."

Lucy listens without interrupting. When he finishes she looks at the floor for a moment. Her first instinct is clearly to disagree. Her second instinct is that she has spent three days in a construction site and is currently Level 0 with a weapon she has never swung at anything, and that her first instinct has not been doing her any favors.

"Can I come with you?" she asks.

"Of course," Lucas says. He means it without ceremony. Thinking through problems with someone who can respond is practically different from thinking alone, and the last three days have made that gap more obvious than usual. He is not a naturally social person — the people at construction sites had mostly kept their distance and he had generally found this fine — but conversation with a purpose is different from socializing.

He gets to his feet. She is eating the last of the sunflower seeds one at a time, working through the end of the bag.

"One more thing," he says. "Don't drink alcohol. We're both minors, and it's bad for the liver regardless. But if the theory about the KTV is right — people who were drinking when the system announced became zombies — then alcohol is a real risk if there's ever another system event."

Lucy chews the last sunflower seed carefully. "Yes," she says. "I understand." She does not mention that she had not been drinking at the party. She decides, in the space between his sentence and her answer, that she will keep this detail for a better moment.

"Done?" Lucas asks.

"Done," she says.

He picks up the spear. She picks up the katana with both hands and adjusts her grip until it feels less awkward, which it still does. They go out through the white building's door into the afternoon sun and Lucas turns toward the city without pausing.
